summary: Paul Graham argues that returns for performance are often superlinear, driven by exponential growth and thresholds, so small differences in performance can lead to outsized outcomes. He claims this pattern is fundamental across business, learning, fame, and discovery, and that understanding it is crucial for ambitious people. He also suggests the modern decline of institutional gatekeeping is expanding opportunities for superlinear returns, with higher inequality as a consequence.
key_points: Superlinear returns are a fundamental feature of the world, not an artifact of rules, and appear in business, fame, power, and knowledge.
Two main causes drive superlinear returns: exponential growth and thresholds; many situations combine both.
Learning compounds and is the most reliable path to superlinear returns; always be learning and follow curiosity.
The erosion of institutional gatekeeping increases variance in outcomes, creating more opportunity and more inequality.
To benefit, do exceptionally good work, pick problems that compound, and take risks early to get multiple shots.
money_quotes: You can't understand the world without understanding the concept of superlinear returns.
Whenever how well you do depends on how well you've done, you'll get exponential growth.
If you're not learning, you're probably not on a path that leads to superlinear returns.
Superlinear returns imply inequality. The steeper the return curve, the greater the variation in outcomes.
When in doubt, follow your curiosity. It never lies, and it knows more than you do about what's worth paying attention to.
key_insight: Superlinear returns arise from compounding and thresholds, so the best strategy is to pursue work that accelerates learning and growth, even at the cost of higher risk and inequality.
